supersedes: vocab_coach_topic_bank_v1.3.0 (and v1.2.0, v1.1.0, v1.0.0) -- all kept in place, not deleted or overwritten

scope_note_changelog.change: Initial hand-curated bank: 92 items, 4 topics (environment, technology, education, crime_or_health merged), collocations only.
Added noun_phrase and phrasal_verb item types alongside collocations.

Expanded to 610 items / 10 topics (crime_or_health split into crime_and_justice + health; added government_and_economy, work_and_employment, media_and_communication, urbanization_and_housing, tourism_and_travel). Added cefr_estimate/difficulty_tier and registry_source fields.

Full revision per VOCAB_COACH_BANK_REVISION_PROMPT_V1.md audit (10 problems). (1) Corrected ~20+ mistagged cefr_estimate words via a manual override layer cross-checked against standard EVP/Oxford CEFR conventions (see METHODOLOGY.md for the important caveat on how this was sourced -- a live EVP/Oxford database fetch was attempted and failed, so this is NOT a live lookup). (2) Rebalanced CEFR distribution: added B1-C1 items concentrated in the three topics that were furthest below the 60% B1+ floor (work_and_employment, media_and_communication -- education was addressed via its new subtopic structure, see (10)). Every topic (and every non-general subtopic) now clears 60% B1+. (3) Deduplicated on (headword, collocate, relation_type) instead of raw phrase string -- removed 9 near-duplicate pairs, applied bank-wide including across all subtopics. (4) Rebalanced media_and_communication's type mix from 82%/15%/4% (collocation/noun_phrase/phrasal_verb) to 63%/31%/6%, in line with the other topics. (5) This changelog-style scope_note plus vocab_coach_topic_bank_v1_3_0_METHODOLOGY.md now actually deliver what earlier versions' 'see addendum' references promised. (6) confidence field renamed to pattern_type_confidence, documented plainly as a pattern-type constant (it takes only 3 values, tied to relation_type, not a real per-item quality score) rather than left implying item-level signal it didn't carry. (7)-(9) apply to the task-type bank, see vocab_coach_task_type_bank_v1.2.0. (10) Introduced subtopics for the 4 topics found genuinely heterogeneous (environment, technology, health, education) -- see subtopics_index/subtopics structure. The other 6 topics were judged reasonably homogeneous already and left flat, per the prompt's explicit instruction not to force a uniform split.

Per Topic_Academic_Vocab_Expansion_Spec_v1.docx: (1) Added 8 new topics -- society_and_ageing, family_and_relationships, globalization_and_international_relations (Tier 1 priority); sport_and_leisure, science_and_space, food_and_diet (Tier 2); transportation, language_and_culture (Tier 3) -- each with its own heterogeneous subcategories (subtopics_index/subtopics), matching the schema of the 4 topics already restructured in v1.3.0, not the flat 6. (2) Added Tier B (topic-anchored academic collocation) content to ALL 18 topics (10 existing + 8 new): a new 'academic_collocations' subtopic for the 4 already-subtopic'd topics (old: environment/technology/health/education; new: all 8), and academic_collocation-typed items appended directly into the 6 flat topics' existing item lists (crime_and_justice, government_and_economy, work_and_employment, media_and_communication, urbanization_and_housing, tourism_and_travel) -- flat topics were NOT restructured into subtopics, preserving v1.3.0's explicit judgement that they are reasonably homogeneous. (3) arts_and_funding: decided as a borderline, low-priority (Tier 3) case folded into government_and_economy's existing flat items list rather than a new top-level topic or subtopic (see addendum for reasoning). (4) Coverage-check supplements per spec §1.3: youth_unemployment items added to work_and_employment; remote/distance-learning items added to education's teaching_methods_and_technology subtopic -- neither given a new top-level topic, per the spec's own recommendation. (5) All new content sourced via manual curation, NOT a fake registry_source citation -- see SOURCING NOTE in build_topic_bank_v1_4_0 scripts and the addendum for why (spec §4 found the existing registry_source citations don't resolve to a real file in this project).

known_limitations: cefr_estimate reflects single hardest-word difficulty only, not collocation-pairing production difficulty (two common words can still form a non-obvious pairing a learner gets wrong) -- no pairing-naturalness signal was available in the registries to use as a secondary check. Downstream consumers should not over-trust cefr_estimate as a full difficulty measure.

The CEFR correction layer (Problem 1) is manually assigned against general EVP/Oxford conventions, not a live database cross-check -- a live fetch was attempted (3 timeouts, see METHODOLOGY.md) and abandoned per this session's tooling policy rather than routed around.

Final overall CEFR distribution (A1 9.1% / A2 16.3% / B1 23.2% / B2 34.7% / C1 15.2% / C2 1.4%) is substantially less A1/A2-heavy than v1.2.0 (was A1 15.7%/A2 26.4%) and every topic now clears the 60% B1+ floor, but does not hit the prompt's exact target shape (esp. C1+C2 combined is 16.6% vs. a ~25% target) -- genuinely-advanced, registry-attested vocabulary for these specific topics is finite; reaching the exact target would require either lower-confidence extractions or non-registry-sourced content, both trade-offs not made here.

environment::waste_and_recycling has only 6 items (below the 15-25 subtopic target) -- the canonical registries have no further clean, real headwords for this specific subtopic beyond 'waste' itself; documented rather than padded with lower-quality filler.

'general' buckets (one per restructured topic) are deliberately small and were NOT held to the 60% B1+ floor or 15-25 size target -- per the revision prompt's own framing, general is 'not a subtopic in the classification sense, just a shared pool', so it was treated as an explicit exception rather than forced to fit subtopic-level targets.

New-topic and Tier B item counts are a first pass, not calibrated to the 15-25-item-per-subtopic target v1.3.0 used for its 4 restructured topics -- several new subtopics (e.g. transportation's academic_collocations at 4 items, language_and_culture's general at 3 items) are below that range, documented rather than padded with filler, consistent with v1.3.0's own precedent (environment::waste_and_recycling was left at 6 items for the same reason).

registry_source values for all v1.4.0 content are honest manual-curation tags, not live registry file pointers -- see spec §4's finding that the pre-existing registry_source citations (e.g. 'positive_collocations_registry.tsv:...') do not resolve to any real file in this project either. New content does not repeat that citation style.

The new relation_type 'academic_verb_object' (Tier B / academic_collocation items) uses a pattern_type_confidence constant of 0.75, lower than the pre-existing 0.82-0.92 constants -- chosen deliberately to signal manual-curation provenance rather than registry-extraction, per the project's Problem-6 fix (pattern_type_confidence must not imply a false per-item signal).

Tier 1/2/3 topic-priority ordering and the exact new-topic item counts are reasoned from IELTS Task 2 topic frequency and this project's own prompt bank, not a formal frequency count across a large real prompt corpus -- exactly the caveat the spec itself states in §5.

The CEFR/register B2+ quality bar for academic_collocation items (spec §2.3) is applied by manual judgement cross-checked against Oxford 3000/5000 and EVP conventions, not a live database lookup -- same caveat and same methodology as v1.3.0's CEFR correction layer.

subtopics_index.climate_change: Global warming, greenhouse gas emissions, extreme weather (drought/flood).
